title: The Macro Minute: Is the stock market underpricing a deterioration in the US-Israel-Iran ceasefire?

Happy Thursday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, with our
macro minute for Thursday, April 16th,
2026. Hope everyone's having a great
week. So, as always, we start with the
executive summary from today's lead off
morning note. Let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, is the
stock market underpricing a
deterioration in the US-Israel-Iran
ceasefire?
Our answer is, from a short-term
perspective, yes, at least according to
our crowding model.
Other elements of our fundamental
research summary and quantitative risk
management summary that have longer
forecasting horizons continue to signal
blue skies ahead for {quote} dumb money
bulls and storm clouds for {quote} smart
money bears.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis. Starting with
the growth cycle. The latest initial and
continuing jobless claims data currently
signal a low probability of a developing
recession in the US economy, as do the
42 macro fat five recession signaling
indicators and 42 macro business cycle
model.
The April uh New York Fed Services
Sector Survey and March industrial
production and capacity utilization data
supported our resilient US economy and
paradigm C themes. For those who may be
new, paradigm C is the growth phase of
the cut-grow-print sequence required to
address the geopolitically driven
supply-demand imbalance in the Treasury
bond market, which has been our core uh
research view uh since the summer of
2023. Uh the April New York Fed Services
Sector Survey data did not support our
jobless recovery theme.
In China, the Q1 GDP data outperformed
our expectation for firming growth in
Q1, and the March activity and property
supply-demand data generally supported
our outlook for firming growth over the
medium term. In the UK, the February GDP
and industrial production data supported
our outlook for moderating growth.
Regarding the inflation cycle, the April
New York Fed Services Sector Survey data
supported our sticky inflation and
cooling housing and labor themes. In
China, the March property price data
supported our outlook for moderating
inflation. In the Eurozone, the final
March CPI data supported our outlook for
moderating inflation over the medium
term. Nothing actionable with respect to
our Bayesian priors from the monetary
policy cycle or the fiscal policy cycle.
Regarding the liquidity cycle, recall
that the PBOC has been the world's most
aggressive major central bank in terms
of monetary accommodation in recent
months. The PBOC's quiet balance sheet
expansion is why Chinese liquidity is
expanding nearly 20% on a three-month
annualized basis, which compares
extremely favorably to the Eurozone at
down 6%, Japan at down 7%,
Switzerland at down 3%, the UK at down
7%, and the US at up only 1%. Investors
should keep buying what the Chinese are
buying with all this excess liquidity,
i.e. gold and international ex-US.
And and finally, positioning cycle,
Pakistan is intensifying diplomatic
engagement to extend the US-Iran
ceasefire beyond its scheduled
expiration next week in order to secure
additional runway for negotiating a
doable peace accord. US-Iran officials
are actively evaluating a two-week
ceasefire extension amid mutual
reluctance to reinitiate hostilities
while negotiating unresolved
flashpoints, including including Strait
of Hormuz access and Iran's nuclear
missile programs. These developments are
consistent with our sanguine take on the
conflict's resolution. Refer to my
Bloomberg surveillance appearance from
last Wednesday, April 8th, for more
details on that view.
Uh regarding uh transitioning to our 42
macro dashboard, as always, we wrap up
with a question from our community. This
one's titled market action over the past
two weeks. Says, "Darius and community,
I've been over 30 years of following the
markets. I've never seen this type of
market action. The S&P and Nasdaq made a
low on March 30th and then has proceeded
to go parabolic and run up 15% in the
last two weeks, making a new all-time
high with no letup and huge unfilled
gaps. This is abnormal market behavior
from where I sit. Uh generally, the
market doesn't rally that way unless
it's coming off of a major market
bottom, in which capitulation selling
has already taken place. Uh this was not
that. It was relatively minor selloff
from a high due to inflation fears, the
Iran war, geopolitics, and resulting
rise in energy prices. I fear that the
gains are not sustainable, as the market
seems to be heavily emotionally driven
at the moment. While I understand that
following kiss and Dr. Mo is the way to
go, I have a difficult time reconciling
such abnormal behavior and sleeping
comfortably uh at night. So, excellent
question. I think a lot of investors out
there are sort of feel uh this way about
the rapid recovery we've seen in
markets, particularly in the context of
not having, you know, an official,
durable end to the US-Israel-Iran
conflict. And then more importantly, the
conflict is less relevant, but what
really matters, as we've talked in this
program and other programs, the
liquidity uh uh the the the the hit to
liquidity from the uh restricted
supplies of petroleum products and
restricted supplies of fertilizer
inputs, uh that hit to global liquidity
is the big issue uh in terms of
in terms of asset markets. And so, the
fact that it's not yet truly resolved uh
has a lot of investors feeling angst
about where to from here in terms of the
speed with which the markets have
recovered.
So, the first thing I'll say is, markets
did have a pretty meaningful correction.
Uh if you go this chart from Bloomberg
this morning, they show the Bloomberg
Magnificent Seven index,
uh you know, at one point it was down
15-16%
uh year-to-date
um prior to this rapid recovery that
we've seen over the past a couple of
weeks. And in our view, this rapid
recovery makes a lot of sense from a
fundamental perspective. Recall that
when you look at our active fundamental
themes from our fundamental research
summary, paradigm resilient US economy,
paradigm C, productivity boom, jobless
recovery, and cooling housing and labor
are all bullish themes. Uh we only have
one bearish theme, which is sticky
inflation, and our source of funds theme
is not really bullish or bearish, it's
just about, you know, factor uh
guiding investors to the appropriate
factor tilts, namely to international
small caps and cyclicals. Um and so,
when we, you know, we just broadly scan
our fundamental research summary, the
distribution of probable economic,
market, and policy outcomes has very
clearly shifted to the right. The shape
and the skew of the distribution has
evolved in a bullish manner in recent
weeks. And and and more importantly, in
terms of the speed with which the
markets have recovered, uh there's two
reasons for this, in my opinion. Uh one,
we actually did see a meaningful washout
in crowded bulls' positioning. Recall
that we came into 2025 2026, rather,
highlighting on macro voices and in our
macro scouting report that, "Hey, our
positioning model, which tracks every
market cycle back to 1987, has was
signaling the highest degree of crowded
bulls' positioning ever in that model."
And so, uh we've washed that out by
about a third,
you know,
to to to the current readings right now.
So, that's obviously positive in terms
of what's given the market the ability
to to to continue recovering. But most
importantly, our macro weather model was
very consistently signaling tailwinds
across uh you know,
a handful of the key macro cycles. Uh
right now, the macro weather model has a
modest near-term tailwind for asset
markets. It has a meaningful near-term
tailwind uh from the monetary policy
cycle. It's signaling a meaningful
near-term tailwind from the fiscal
policy cycle. It's signaling a modest
near-term tailwind from the liquidity
cycle. That's a lot of really good stuff
that's been happening and continues to
happen from the perspective of the key
leading indicators of each of those
cycles that our macro weather model was
tracking on a nowcasting basis. So, uh
that's a long way to way of saying we
saw a washout in the positioning that we
were warning about coming into the year.
And by and large, the fundamentals,
separate and apart from the
US-Israel-Iran conflict and the Strait
of Hormuz issues, remain extremely
positive. And so, the markets just
getting back to where they were earlier
in the year, in my opinion, should have
occurred on a very short-term time
horizon. Now, the quick I guess the
question is, where are we headed from
here? And as we highlighted in today's
executive summary and have we, you know,
recently highlighted, you know, dating
back to
last Wednesday,
we think markets are going to leave the
the YouTube bear porn community behind
this year for the fourth consecutive
year in a row and the six of the past
eight years. And so, if we're right on
that, then you know, this will be
another good year. So, you know, you got
to plant your flag, you got to take risk
to make money in financial markets. And
if we're wrong on that, then kiss and
Dr. Mo will do what they always do,
which is risk manage better than I can
as a fundamental oriented investor. So,
we're wrapping up there. Darius Dale
here presenting our macro minute for
Thursday, April 16th, 2026. Best of luck
out there today. We'll catch you back
here tomorrow. Cheers.
